reasoning:
Strong overall design with complete coverage of all required sections, clear right-sized architecture, concrete deployment guidance, and explicit trade-offs. It is notably practical for a single VPS (Node.js + WebSocket + SQLite + Docker Compose + nginx) and includes operational details (health checks, backups, monitoring). Invite lifecycle is well covered end-to-end (creation, hashing, single-use, expiry, audit chain, revocation, abuse controls), though there are inconsistencies (it says token is stored hashed-only, but schema also stores plaintext invite_id). Threat model quality is high: it clearly distinguishes metadata visibility vs content secrecy, and addresses malicious server operator, DB exfiltration, device compromise, and invite limitations.

Main weakness is cryptographic precision/soundness: it references proven building blocks (X3DH + Double Ratchet) but mixes key types inconsistently (Ed25519 identity key then using IK in X25519 ECDH), has confused DH step definitions, omits one-time prekeys, and uses a simplified group symmetric key model without true forward secrecy. It also accepts no safety-number verification by default, leaving initial key-substitution risk. These are meaningful issues, though the server is still generally designed to store ciphertext-only and be unable to directly read content. Data model and real-time protocol are detailed and actionable, but include some rough/contradictory notes.
